text.title: accessibility Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for accessibility. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use semantic HTML elements (`header`, `nav`, `main`, `article`, `button`, `label`, `fieldset`) before reaching for ARIA. First rule of ARIA: don't use ARIA.
rule.rationale: Native elements carry built-in semantics, keyboard behavior, and screen-reader support. ARIA can only add or override semantics; it cannot fix non-semantic markup.

rule.statement: Use a single `<h1>` per page; never skip heading levels. Maintain heading hierarchy h1 → h2 → h3 throughout.
rule.rationale: Screen-reader users navigate by heading level; skipped levels create confusion about the document structure.

rule.statement: Add a visible skip-link to `<main>` at the top of every page.
rule.rationale: Skip-links are cheap and give keyboard users a way to bypass repetitive header navigation on every page load.

rule.statement: Add `aria-label` only to elements whose accessible name is not already provided by visible text or a `<label>`. Never duplicate visible text in `aria-label`.
rule.rationale: Duplicate labels are redundant and confuse screen readers; they also create maintenance overhead.

rule.statement: Never add `aria-hidden="true"` to focusable elements. Remove from tab order with `tabIndex={-1}` first.
rule.rationale: `aria-hidden` on a focusable element hides it from screen readers but not from keyboards, causing a navigation trap.

rule.statement: Use `aria-live="polite"` for status updates; `aria-live="assertive"` only for errors. Never use both on the same node. The live region must be in the DOM before content is inserted.
rule.rationale: Inserting a live region with text already inside it does not trigger an announcement — the region must exist first.

rule.statement: Every interactive element must be keyboard accessible. Tab order must match visual order. Never use `outline: none` without a `:focus-visible` replacement that meets 3:1 contrast against the adjacent color.
rule.rationale: Removing focus indicators without replacement makes keyboard navigation impossible for users who cannot use a mouse.

rule.statement: Modals must: trap focus within the dialog, restore focus to the trigger on close, set `aria-modal="true"`, label the dialog with `aria-labelledby`, and close on Escape.
rule.rationale: Without focus trapping, screen reader users and keyboard users can interact with content behind the modal. Without restore, they lose their place after dismissal.

rule.statement: Composite widgets (tabs, listboxes, tree menus) must use roving `tabIndex` (set to 0 for the active item, -1 for the rest) and arrow-key navigation.
rule.rationale: WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices defines the expected keyboard contract for each widget type; deviating confuses screen reader users.

rule.statement: Touch targets minimum 48×48 CSS pixels on mobile.
rule.rationale: Smaller targets are difficult to hit accurately, especially for users with motor impairments.

rule.statement: Normal text: minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio. Large text (18px+ or 14px+ bold): 3:1. UI components and focus indicators: 3:1. Check dark mode separately.
rule.rationale: WCAG 2.1 AA contrast minima; below these thresholds text is unreadable for users with low vision or in challenging lighting.

rule.statement: Never use color as the sole means of conveying information. Always pair a color indicator with an icon, text label, or pattern.
rule.rationale: Users with color blindness cannot distinguish red from green; color-only error indicators are invisible to them.

rule.statement: Every `<input>` must have a programmatic label — `<label htmlFor>` or `aria-labelledby`. Placeholders are not labels.
rule.rationale: Placeholders disappear when the user starts typing, leaving them without context for what the field expects.

rule.statement: Use `fieldset` and `legend` for radio button and checkbox groups.
rule.rationale: Fieldset + legend provides a programmatic group label and description for grouped controls.

rule.statement: On form validation failure: surface an error summary via `role="alert"`, move focus to it, and link each error to its field via `aria-describedby`.
rule.rationale: Screen readers announce `role="alert"` automatically; without focus move, keyboard users land at the submit button and cannot see the errors.

rule.statement: Add `aria-invalid="true"` and `aria-required` to form fields as their state changes.
rule.rationale: Dynamic state indicators help screen-reader users understand the current field validity and requirement state.

rule.statement: Meaningful alt text on informational images (not generic "image of..."). Decorative images: `alt=""` with `role="presentation"`.
rule.rationale: Screen-reader users depend on alt text for context; generic alt text is useless. Decorative images should be hidden.

rule.statement: Complex images: use `aria-describedby` pointing to a `<figcaption>` with full description.
rule.rationale: Simple alt text cannot convey complex information (charts, diagrams); a longer description adjacent to the image is required.

rule.statement: Video: include `<track kind="captions">` with both language options.
rule.rationale: Captions serve deaf/hard-of-hearing users and non-native speakers; multilingual options support internationalization.

rule.statement: Audio: provide a transcript link adjacent to the player.
rule.rationale: Transcripts make audio content searchable, referenceable, and accessible to deaf users and non-native speakers.

rule.statement: Honour `prefers-reduced-motion` in every animation: wrap transitions in `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` or use `useReducedMotion()` in framer-motion.
rule.rationale: Uncontrolled motion can trigger vestibular disorders; this media query is the standard mechanism for respecting user preferences.

rule.statement: Treat axe-core failures as build failures: set `categories:accessibility >= 0.95` in Lighthouse CI and fail the build. Automated tools catch 30–40% of issues; supplement with keyboard-only and screen-reader manual testing.
rule.rationale: Automated gating catches regressions continuously; manual testing finds the majority of issues that automation misses.

rule.statement: jest-axe: include `expect(await axe(container)).toHaveNoViolations()` in every component unit test.
rule.rationale: Component-level testing catches issues early; combined with route-level testing, you get both deep and wide coverage.

rule.statement: Test keyboard-only navigation in E2E (Tab through page, assert focus order and reachability of all interactive elements).
rule.rationale: Keyboard navigation is the #1 area missed by automated tools; E2E testing ensures order and reachability.

rule.statement: Manual check: test with at least one screen reader (NVDA on Win, VoiceOver on macOS).
rule.rationale: Screen-reader behavior varies; manual testing on a real tool catches nuances that automation cannot.

rule.statement: Add `lang` attribute to `<html>`; update on language switch. Update `dir="rtl"` or `dir="ltr"` when switching to RTL languages.
rule.rationale: Screen readers use the lang attribute to select voices and pronunciation; dir specifies text directionality for proper layout of RTL languages.
